audioscript.text: Part 1, you will hear a man phoning to inquire about a cleaning service for his apartment.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Easy Life Cleaning Services, Jacinta speaking.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: Oh, hello.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: I'm looking for a cleaning service for my apartment.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: Do you do domestic cleaning?
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: Sure.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: Well, it's just a 1 bedroom flat.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: Do you have a basic cleaning package?
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: Yes, for a 1 bedroom flat, we're probably looking at about 2 hours for a clean.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: So we'd do a thorough clean of all surfaces in each room, and polish them where necessary.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: Does your apartment have carpets?
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: No, I don't have any, but the floor would need cleaning.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: Of course, we'd do that in every room. And we'd do a thorough clean of the kitchen and bathroom.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: Then we have some additional services, which you can request if you want,
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: so for example, we can clean your oven for you every week.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: Actually, I hardly ever use that, but can you do the fridge?
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: Sure, would you like that done every week?
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: Yes, definitely.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: And would ironing clothes be an additional service you can do?
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: It wouldn't be much, just my shirts for work that week.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: That's fine, and we could also clean your microwave if you want.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: No, I wipe that out pretty regularly, so there's no need for that.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: We also offer additional services that you might want a bit less often, say every month.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: So for example, if the inside of your windows need cleaning, we could do that.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: Yes, that'd be good.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: I'm on the 15th floor, so the outside gets done regularly by specialists, but the inside does get a bit grubby.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: And we could arrange for your curtains to get cleaned if necessary.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: No, they're OK.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: But would you be able to do something about the balcony?
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: It's quite small, and I don't use it much, but it could do with a wash every month or so.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: Yes, we can get the pressure washer onto that.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, if you're interested, we do offer some other possibilities to do with general maintenance.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: For example, if you have a problem with water, and you need a plumber in a hurry,
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: we can put you in touch with a reliable one who can come out straightaway. And the same thing if you need an electrician.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: Right, that's good to know.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: I've only just moved here, so I don't have any of those sorts of contacts.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: And I don't know if this is of interest to you, but we also offer a special vacuum cleaning system, which can improve the indoor air quality of your home by capturing up to 99% of all the dust in the air.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: So, if you're troubled by allergies, this can make a big difference.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: In fact, I don't have that sort of problem, but I'll bear it in mind.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: Now, can you tell me a bit about your cleaning staff?
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: Of course, so all our cleaners are very carefully selected.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: When they apply to us, they have to undergo a security check with the police to make sure they don't have any sort of criminal background.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: And of course they have to provide references as well.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: Then if we think they might be suitable for the job, we give them training for it.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: That lasts for two weeks, so it's very thorough.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: And at the end of it, they have a test.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: If they pass that, we take them on, but we monitor them very carefully.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: We ask all our clients to complete a review of their performance after every visit.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: And to email it to us, so we can pick up any problems straightaway and deal with them.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: OK, well, that all sounds good.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: And will I always have the same cleaner?
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: We do our best to organize it that way, and we usually manage it.
audioscript.speaker: JACINTA

audioscript.text: Good, that's fine.
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: Right, so I'd like to go ahead and...
audioscript.speaker: CLIENT

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Part 2, you will hear a training manager giving a talk to a group of senior staff from a hotel chain about the problem of high staff turnover.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: As many of you here today have worked in the hotel industry for some time, I'm sure you have experienced the problem of high staff turnover in your hotels.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Every hotel relies on having loyal and experienced members of staff who make sure that everything runs smoothly.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If staff are constantly changing it can make life difficult for everyone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But why do staff leave frequently in many hotels?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Of course, many hotel jobs such as cleaning are low skilled and are not well paid.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A lot of managers think it's this, and the long hours that are the main causes of high staff turnover, but what they don't realize is that it's the lack of training in many hotel jobs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which is a huge factor.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, what kind of problems does a high turnover of staff cause?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, having to recruit new staff all the time can be very time consuming.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And managers may have to cover some duties while waiting for new staff to arrive.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This means they don't have time to think about less immediate problems, such as how to improve their service.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When staff leave, it can also severely affect the colleagues they leave behind.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It has a negative effect on remaining staff, who may start to feel that they too should be thinking about leaving.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So what can be done to change this situation?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Firstly, managers should stop making basic errors, which leave their staff feeling upset and resentful.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When organizing shifts, for example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Make sure you never give certain staff preferential treatment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: All staff should be given some choice about when they work, and everyone should have to work some evening and weekend shifts.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you treat staff fairly, they'll be more likely to step in and help when extra staff are needed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Keeping staff happy has other tangible benefits for the business.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Take the Dunwich Hotel as an example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It had been experiencing a problem with staff complaints, and in order to deal with this, invested in staff training and improved staff conditions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Not only did the level of complaints fall, but they also noticed a significant increase in the amount each customer spent during their stay.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They have now introduced a customer loyalty scheme, which is going really well.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, I'd like to look at some ways you can reduce staff turnover in your hotels, and I'll do this by giving some examples of hotels where I've done some training recently.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Sun Club received feedback which showed that staff thought managers didn't value their opinions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They weren't made to feel they were partners who were contributing to the success of the business as a whole.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This situation has changed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Junior staff at all levels are regularly invited to meetings where their ideas are welcomed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A year ago, The Portland recognized the need to invest in staff retention.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Their first step was to introduce a scheme for recognizing talent amongst their employees.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The hope is that organizing training for individuals with management potential will encourage them to stay with the business.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At Bluewater, managers decided to recognize 50 high achievers from across the company's huge hotel chain as a reward.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They're sent on an all-expenses-paid trip abroad every year.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Fun is an important element in the trips, but there's also the opportunity to learn something useful.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This year's trip included a visit to a brewery, where staff learned about the new beer that would be served in the hotel.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Pentlow Hotels identified that retention of junior reception staff was an issue in order to encourage them to see that working in a hotel could be worthwhile and rewarding, with good prospects, they introduced a management program.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These staff were given additional responsibilities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the chance to work in various roles in the hotel.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Green Planet wanted to be seen as a caring employer. To make life easier for staff, many of whom had childcare responsibilities,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: the hotel began issuing vouchers to help cover the cost of childcare.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Louise Marsh at the Amesbury has one of the best staff retention rates in the business.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Since she joined the company, she's made a huge effort to achieve this.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: By creating a co-operative and supportive environment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For her, the staff are part of a large family where everyone is valued.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: OK, now I'd like to...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Since she joined the company, she's made a huge effort to achieve this.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: By creating a co-operative and supportive environment.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For her, the staff are part of a large family where everyone is valued.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: OK, now I'd like to...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Many hotel managers are unaware that their staff often leave because of
What is the impact of high staff turnover on managers?
What mistake should managers always avoid?
options: neglecting to have enough staff during busy periods

items.title: What unexpected benefit did Dunwich Hotel notice after improving staff retention rates?
options: an increase in loyalty club membership
a rise in spending per customer

text: Which way of reducing staff turnover was used in each of the following hotels?

The Portland     <input>

body.title: Hotels

audioscript.text: Part 3, you will hear two students called Jeanne and Thomas discussing their sport science course.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Thomas.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: How are you enjoying the course so far?
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Yeah, I think it's good.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Remind me why did you decide to study sports science?
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Didn't you want to be a professional athlete when you were at school?
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Yeah, that was my goal, and all my classmates assumed I would achieve it.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: They thought I was brilliant.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: That must have been a nice feeling.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Hmm, I thought I could win anything.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: There was no one who could run faster than me.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: So what happened?
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Did your mom and dad want you to be more academic?
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Not at all.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Perhaps they should have pushed me harder, though.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: What do you mean?
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: I think I should have practiced more.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: What makes you say that?
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Well, I went out to Kenya for a couple of weeks to train.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Really!
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: I didn't know that.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: I was chosen to go there out of loads of kids and run with some of the top teenage athletes in the world.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: And I was so calm about it.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: I just kept thinking how fortunate I was, what a great chance this was.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Everyone back home was so proud of me.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: But once we started competing, I very quickly realized I wasn't good enough.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Hmm, that must have been a huge shock.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: I thought 'this can't be happening'!
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: I was used to winning.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: I'm sorry to hear that.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: It's OK.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: I'm over it now.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: And I think it's much better to do a university course.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: And this one has such a variety of sports related areas.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: It's going to be good.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Oh, I agree.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: I chose it because of that.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, Jeanne, have you thought of any ideas for the discussion session next week on technology and sport?
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: We have to cover more than one sport, don't we?
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: You know, we always think technology is about the future, but we could gather some ideas about past developments in sport.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Look at early types of equipment, perhaps.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Uh. I remember reading something about table tennis bats once, how they ended up being covered with pimpled rubber.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Cos they were just wooden at first, I'd imagine.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: In about the 1920s, a factory was making rolls of the rubber in bulk, for something like horse harnesses.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Really!
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Yeah, and someone realized that it would make a perfect covering for the wooden bats.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: So, what about cricket?
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: That's had a few innovative changes, maybe the pads they wear on their legs.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: I don't think they've changed much, but I'm just looking on the internet.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: And it says that when the first cricket helmet came in 1978, the Australian batsman who first wore it was booed and jeered by people watching because it was so ugly!
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Wow, players have to protect themselves from getting hurt.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: I mean everyone wears one now.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Hmm, unlike the cycle helmet, well unless you're a professional, but you're right.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Many ordinary bikers don't wear a helmet.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Hey, look at these pictures of original helmet designs.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: This one looks like an upside-down bowl.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Yet the woman's laughing.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Ha, she's so proud to be wearing it.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: It says serious cyclists ended up with wet hair from all the hard exercise.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: I guess that's why they have large air vents in them now, so that the skin can breathe more easily.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: OK, so we've done helmets.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: What about golf balls?
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Or better still, golf clubs.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: They've changed a lot.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Yeah, I remember my great grandfather telling me that because a club was made entirely of wood.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: It would easily break, and players had to get another.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: There's no wood at all in them now, is there?
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: No, they're much more powerful.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: The same must be true of hockey sticks.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Hmm. I don't think so, because players still use wooden sticks today.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Hmm. What it does say here though, is that when the game started, you had to produce a stick yourself.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: I guess they just weren't being manufactured.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: So one more perhaps, what about football?
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Well, I know the first balls were made of animal skin.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Yeah, they covered them with pieces of leather that were stitched together.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: The balls let in water when it rained.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Oh, that would have made them much heavier.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: You can imagine the damage to players' necks when the ball was headed.
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: Oh, how painful that must have been.
audioscript.speaker: JEANNE

audioscript.text: Yeah, well, I think we can put together some useful ideas...
audioscript.speaker: THOMAS

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Part 4, you will hear a presentation by a food science student about the production of maple syrup.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: For first, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, everyone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Today we're going to look at another natural food product, and that's maple syrup.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What is this exactly?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, maple syrup looks rather like clear honey, but it's not made by bees.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's produced from the plant fluid or sap.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Inside the maple tree, and that makes maple syrup a very natural product.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Maple syrup is a thick, golden, sweet-tasting liquid.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That can be bought in bottles or jars, and poured onto foods such as waffles and ice cream, or used in the baking of cakes and pastries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It contains no preservatives or added ingredients, and it provides a healthy alternative to refined sugar.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let's just talk a bit about the maple tree itself, which is where maple syrup comes from.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, there are many species of maple tree.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And they'll grow without fertilizer in areas where there's plenty of moisture in the soil.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, they'll only do this if another important criterion is fulfilled.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which is that they must have full or partial sun exposure during the day and very cool nights, and I'll talk more about that in a minute.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are only certain parts of the world that provide all these conditions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One is Canada, and by that, I mean all parts of Canada.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the other is the northeastern states of North America.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In these areas, the climate suits the trees perfectly.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, Canada produces over two thirds of the world's maple syrup.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which is why the five pointed maple leaf is a Canadian symbol, and has featured on the flag since 1964.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So how did maple syrup production begin?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, long before Europeans settled in these parts of the world, the indigenous communities had started producing maple sugar.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They bored holes in the trunks of maple trees, and used containers made of tree bark to collect the liquid sap as it poured out.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As they were unable to keep the liquid for any length of time, they didn't have storage facilities in those days.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They boiled the liquid by placing pieces of rock that had become scorching hot from the sun.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Into the sap.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They did this until it turned into sugar, and they were then able to use this to sweeten their food and drinks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Since that time,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: improvements have been made to the process, but it has changed very little overall.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, let's look at the production of maple syrup today.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Clearly, the maple forests are a valuable resource in many Canadian and North American communities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The trees have to be well looked after, and they cannot be used to make syrup until the trunks reach a diameter of around 25 cm.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This can take anything up to 40 years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As I've already mentioned, maple trees need the right conditions to grow, and also to produce sap.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Why is this?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, what happens is that during a cold night, the tree absorbs water from the soil, and that rises through the tree's vascular system.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But then in the warmer daytime, the change in temperature causes the water to be pushed back down to the bottom of the tree.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This continual movement up and down leads to the formation of the sap, needed for maple syrup production.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When the tree is ready, it can be tapped, and this involves drilling a small hole into the trunk, and inserting a tube into it that ends in a bucket.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The trees can often take several taps, though the workers take care not to cause any damage to the healthy growth of the tree itself.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The sap that comes out of the trees consists of 98% water and 2% sugar and other nutrients.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It has to be boiled, so that much of that water evaporates.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And this process has to take place immediately, using what are called evaporators.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These are basically extremely large pans.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The sap is poured into these, a fire is built, and the pans are then heated until the sap boils.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As it does this, the water evaporates.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the syrup begins to form.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The evaporation process creates large quantities of steam, and the sap becomes thicker and denser, and at just the right moment, when the sap is thick enough to be called maple syrup, the worker removes it from the heat.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: After this process, something called sugar sand has to be filtered out, as this builds up during the boiling and gives the syrup a cloudy appearance and a slightly gritty taste.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Once this has been done, the syrup is ready to be packaged, so that it can be used for a whole variety of products.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It takes 40 liters of sap to produce 1 liter of maple syrup.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So you can get an idea of how much is needed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So that's the basic process.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In places like Quebec where...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Part 4, you will hear a presentation by a food science student about the production of maple syrup.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: For first, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Hello, everyone.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Today we're going to look at another natural food product, and that's maple syrup.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What is this exactly?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, maple syrup looks rather like clear honey, but it's not made by bees.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's produced from the plant fluid or sap.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Inside the maple tree, and that makes maple syrup a very natural product.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Maple syrup is a thick, golden, sweet-tasting liquid.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That can be bought in bottles or jars, and poured onto foods such as waffles and ice cream, or used in the baking of cakes and pastries.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It contains no preservatives or added ingredients, and it provides a healthy alternative to refined sugar.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Let's just talk a bit about the maple tree itself, which is where maple syrup comes from.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, there are many species of maple tree.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And they'll grow without fertilizer in areas where there's plenty of moisture in the soil.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, they'll only do this if another important criterion is fulfilled.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Which is that they must have full or partial sun exposure during the day and very cool nights, and I'll talk more about that in a minute.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are only certain parts of the world that provide all these conditions.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One is Canada, and by that, I mean all parts of Canada.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And the other is the northeastern states of North America.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In these areas, the climate suits the trees perfectly.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In fact, Canada produces over two thirds of the world's maple syrup.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Which is why the five pointed maple leaf is a Canadian symbol, and has featured on the flag since 1964.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So how did maple syrup production begin?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, long before Europeans settled in these parts of the world, the indigenous communities had started producing maple sugar.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They bored holes in the trunks of maple trees, and used containers made of tree bark to collect the liquid sap as it poured out.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As they were unable to keep the liquid for any length of time, they didn't have storage facilities in those days.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They boiled the liquid by placing pieces of rock that had become scorching hot from the sun.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Into the sap.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They did this until it turned into sugar, and they were then able to use this to sweeten their food and drinks.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Since that time,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: improvements have been made to the process, but it has changed very little overall.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, let's look at the production of maple syrup today.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Clearly, the maple forests are a valuable resource in many Canadian and North American communities.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The trees have to be well looked after, and they cannot be used to make syrup until the trunks reach a diameter of around 25 cm.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This can take anything up to 40 years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As I've already mentioned, maple trees need the right conditions to grow, and also to produce sap.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Why is this?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, what happens is that during a cold night, the tree absorbs water from the soil, and that rises through the tree's vascular system.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But then in the warmer daytime, the change in temperature causes the water to be pushed back down to the bottom of the tree.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This continual movement up and down leads to the formation of the sap, needed for maple syrup production.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When the tree is ready, it can be tapped, and this involves drilling a small hole into the trunk, and inserting a tube into it that ends in a bucket.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The trees can often take several taps, though the workers take care not to cause any damage to the healthy growth of the tree itself.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The sap that comes out of the trees consists of 98% water and 2% sugar and other nutrients.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It has to be boiled, so that much of that water evaporates.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And this process has to take place immediately, using what are called evaporators.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These are basically extremely large pans.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The sap is poured into these, a fire is built, and the pans are then heated until the sap boils.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As it does this, the water evaporates.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And the syrup begins to form.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The evaporation process creates large quantities of steam, and the sap becomes thicker and denser, and at just the right moment, when the sap is thick enough to be called maple syrup, the worker removes it from the heat.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: After this process, something called sugar sand has to be filtered out, as this builds up during the boiling and gives the syrup a cloudy appearance and a slightly gritty taste.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Once this has been done, the syrup is ready to be packaged, so that it can be used for a whole variety of products.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It takes 40 liters of sap to produce 1 liter of maple syrup.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So you can get an idea of how much is needed.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So that's the basic process.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In places like Quebec where...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
